title: Red velvet cake
subtitle: Oil-based cocoa cake with cream cheese frosting
excerpt: A fine-crumbed, deeply moist two-layer cake made with rapeseed oil, buttermilk, and a small amount of cocoa. The acid-base chemistry of cocoa and buttermilk produces a dark, faintly red crumb without food colouring. Finished with a thick cream cheese frosting.
sourceNotes: Red velvet cake is documented in American domestic cooking from the late nineteenth century, where the name refers both to the colour and the texture — a fine-grained, velvet-soft crumb produced by the reaction of natural cocoa (rich in anthocyanins) with the acid in buttermilk. American household baking manuals from the early twentieth century, available through the Internet Archive, document the cocoa-buttermilk-bicarbonate combination as producing a naturally reddish crumb before synthetic food colouring became widely available.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers. Defrost at room temperature and frost before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The frosted cake keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Serve at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The oil-based sponge sets more evenly than a butter cake — check at 28 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The fine-grained, soft texture characteristic of a red velvet cake, produced by the combination of oil (rather than butter), buttermilk, and a small amount of cocoa. Oil keeps the crumb moist without the crystalline structure of creamed butter; the buttermilk tenderises the flour proteins; the acid-base reaction produces a close, even grain. The result tears almost like cloth — fine and impossibly soft.

content.text: Red velvet is an oil-based cake: no creaming, no beating of butter, just whisking wet into dry. The oil makes it noticeably moister than a butter cake and the crumb stays soft for three days without refrigeration. The small amount of cocoa does two things: its anthocyanins react with the acid in buttermilk to produce a dark, faintly reddish crumb, and it adds a background chocolate flavour that underpins the cream cheese frosting.

items.prepNote: use natural, non-alkali processed for the colour

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Butter and line two round tins. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. In a separate bowl, whisk the {{eggs}}, {{rapeseed-oil}}, {{buttermilk}}, and the {{vanilla-extract}} for the sponge. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and whisk to a smooth batter — this is the
in the making. Divide evenly between the two tins. Bake for 28–30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.

Beat the {{cream-cheese}} and {{unsalted-butter}} together until smooth. Add the sifted {{icing-sugar}} and the {{vanilla-extract}} for the frosting and beat until thick and spreadable. Refrigerate for 15 minutes if it is too soft to hold its shape.

Place one sponge on a plate. Spread one third of the frosting over the top. Place the second sponge on top and spread the remaining frosting over the top and sides. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set before serving.
